feature: In-memory rate limiter bypassable in serverless/multi-instance deployments
root_cause: lib/rate-limit.ts uses an in-memory Map<string, RequestRecord> (rateLimitStore) to track request counts. In serverless (Vercel) and multi-instance deployments, each execution context has isolated memory. An attacker can bypass the rate limit by distributing requests across instances.

patterns.service_pattern: Supabase PostgreSQL is used for all persistence. Service-role client uses SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY. Returns { data, error } tuples.
patterns.rate_limit_pattern: withRateLimit and withRateLimitForMethod are higher-order functions wrapping handlers. They check IP and/or user ID, call checkRateLimit, return 429 on limit exceeded.
patterns.type_pattern: All database types are in lib/supabase/database.types.ts using Database['public']['Tables'] pattern
patterns.cleanup_pattern: cache.ts uses setInterval for periodic cleanup (5 min). Same pattern should be used for DB cleanup.

existing_implementations.description: In-memory rate limiter in lib/rate-limit.ts using Map<string, RequestRecord>. Sliding window implementation with admin bypass. Rate limit configs per type (auth, api-authenticated, api-public, payment).

solution_approach.strategy: Replace in-memory Map with PostgreSQL-backed sliding window rate limiting using Supabase
solution_approach.architecture: UPSERT-based atomic increment on (identifier, type, window_start) with unique constraint
solution_approach.compatibility: Keep existing public API (withRateLimit, withRateLimitForMethod) unchanged - drop-in replacement
solution_approach.fallback: If DB fails, log error and allow request (fail-open with warning) to avoid breaking availability
solution_approach.cleanup: Supabase pg_cron job or Next.js interval cleanup of expired rows
solution_approach.rls: No RLS needed - service role used server-side only
